findings.description: Off-by-one: 'len(hits) <= self.limit' admits limit+1 requests per window (101 instead of 100), should be '<'.
Client key is taken from the spoofable X-Forwarded-For header, letting an attacker rotate keys to bypass limits or impersonate another client's IP.
check() does an unsynchronized read-modify-write on shared _hits, so concurrent gateway threads race, corrupting the list and overrunning the limit.
defaultdict creates a permanent bucket on every key access, so spoofed distinct X-Forwarded-For values grow _hits without bound (memory-exhaustion DoS) between manual sweep() calls.
hits.pop(0) on a list is O(n) per prune, making check() O(n^2) under bursts; a collections.deque would give O(1) pops.
remaining = self.limit - len(hits) is computed after append, can go negative (e.g. -1 once the off-by-one admits 101 hits) and is off-by-one versus the contract.
current_usage() reads self._hits[key] via defaultdict, mutating state by creating an empty bucket on a nominally read-only query.
Clients lacking both X-Forwarded-For and Remote-Addr collapse into a single 'unknown' bucket, so one noisy peer throttles all anonymous clients.
typing.Optional is used but on Python 3.10+ (per dict[str,list] syntax) 'float | None' is the idiomatic style.
